fr.elements: Main Building; Roof System; Front Driveway; Vehicle Parking Area; Landscaped Area
fr.considerations: Multiple Access Points; Limited Rear Access
fr.recommendations: Improving emergency response effectiveness on the property may involve ensuring that multiple access points are clearly marked and maintained to facilitate easy identification and entry by emergency personnel. Additionally, addressing the limited rear access could enhance overall maneuverability, allowing responders to efficiently reach all areas of the property as needed during an emergency. Ensuring the front driveway and vehicle parking area remain unobstructed could support smooth ingress and egress for emergency vehicles, enhancing emergency operations efficiency. Regular maintenance of landscaped areas could prevent vegetation from encroaching onto pathways, which may support better visibility and straightforward navigation. Improving these aspects could greatly support the overall responder visibility and staging during an emergency.

wildfire.conditions: Scattered Trees; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Open Terrain; Open Terrain; Limited Defensible Space; Dense Tree Canopy; Vegetated Perimeter
wildfire.recommendations: To improve wildfire resilience and property preparedness, increasing separation between the scattered trees and structures might help reduce fuel continuity. Managing vegetation directly adjacent to the structure may enhance defensible space and minimize potential structure exposure. Given the open terrain, maintaining these areas free of excessive combustible vegetation could further reduce opportunities for fire spread. Reducing the density of the tree canopy can potentially improve defensible space around the property, while attention to reducing vegetation density along the perimeter of the property could create essential fuel breaks and further interrupt fire pathways. These actions, in conjunction with ongoing management, could support suppression operations and contribute to long-term landscape resilience.
